Set, Map, WeakSet, WeakMap in JavaScript
content:
Definition:
Set and Map are built-in ES6 data structures that allow storing collections of values in more flexible ways than arrays and objects.

Set:
A Set is a collection of unique values (no duplicates allowed).

Example:
const set = new Set([1, 2, 2, 3]);
console.log(set); // {1, 2, 3}

Common methods:
- add(value)
- delete(value)
- has(value)
- clear()
- size (property)

Example:
set.add(4);
set.has(2); // true

Use cases:
- Removing duplicates from arrays
- Fast lookup of unique values

Map:
A Map is a collection of key-value pairs where keys can be of any type (not only strings).

Example:
const map = new Map();
map.set('name', 'Anna');
map.set(1, 'number key');
map.set({}, 'object key');

Common methods:
- set(key, value)
- get(key)
- has(key)
- delete(key)
- clear()
- size (property)

Example:
map.get('name'); // 'Anna'

Difference between Map and Object:
- Map keys can be any type
- Map preserves insertion order
- Map has better performance for frequent additions/removals

WeakSet:
A WeakSet is similar to Set but only stores objects and holds weak references.

Example:
const ws = new WeakSet();
const obj = {};
ws.add(obj);

Key characteristics:
- Only objects are allowed
- Objects can be garbage collected if no other references exist
- Not iterable

WeakMap:
A WeakMap is similar to Map but keys must be objects and are weakly referenced.

Example:
const wm = new WeakMap();
const key = {};
wm.set(key, 'value');

Key characteristics:
- Keys must be objects
- Automatically removed when key is garbage collected
- Not iterable

Use cases for WeakMap/WeakSet:
- Storing private data
- Avoiding memory leaks

Important Notes:
- Set ensures uniqueness of values
- Map is preferred over Object when keys are not strings or when order matters
- Weak collections are useful when working with object lifecycles

Summary (Interview Answer):
Set and Map are ES6 data structures for storing collections. Set stores unique values, while Map stores key-value pairs with keys of any type. WeakSet and WeakMap are similar but only work with objects and allow garbage collection, which helps prevent memory leaks. They are not iterable and are used for managing object-related data safely.

JavaScript in the Browser (Window, Location, History, User Agent)
content:
Definition:
In the browser environment, JavaScript runs inside a global context provided by the browser. The global object in browsers is called 'window', and it provides access to APIs like location, history, and navigator.

Global Object (window):
The 'window' object is the global object in the browser. All global variables and functions are properties of window.

Example:
var x = 10;
console.log(window.x); // 10

Important properties of window:
- window.document → DOM
- window.location → current URL
- window.history → navigation history
- window.navigator → browser information

Location API:
The location object provides information about the current URL and allows navigation.

Example:
console.log(window.location.href); // full URL
console.log(window.location.hostname);

Navigation:
window.location.href = 'https://example.com'; // redirect
window.location.reload(); // reload page

History API:
The history object allows interaction with the browser's session history.

Example:
window.history.back();
window.history.forward();
window.history.go(-1);

Modern API:
history.pushState({ page: 1 }, '', '/page1');
history.replaceState({ page: 2 }, '', '/page2');

Use cases:
- SPA navigation without page reload
- managing browser history

User Agent & Navigator:
The navigator object provides information about the browser and device.

Example:
console.log(navigator.userAgent);
console.log(navigator.platform);

User agent parsing:
User agent string contains information about browser, OS, and device.

Example:
Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36...

Platform/Browser detection:
Developers can check browser capabilities or platform.

Example:
if (navigator.userAgent.includes('Chrome')) {
  console.log('Chrome browser');
}

Important note:
User agent detection is not reliable and is discouraged.

Better approach:
Feature detection instead of browser detection.

Example:
if ('geolocation' in navigator) {
  // safe to use
}

Summary (Interview Answer):
In the browser, JavaScript runs within the global 'window' object, which provides access to APIs like location, history, and navigator. The location API allows reading and changing the URL, while the history API enables navigation without page reloads. The navigator object provides browser and device information, but feature detection is preferred over user agent parsing for reliability.

title: Page Lifecycle and Critical Rendering Path
content:
Definition:
The page lifecycle describes how a browser loads, parses, and renders a web page. The critical rendering path is the sequence of steps the browser follows to convert HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into pixels on the screen.

Parsing:
Parsing is the process of reading HTML and CSS and converting them into structured data.

Steps:
1. HTML is parsed into the DOM (Document Object Model)
2. CSS is parsed into the CSSOM (CSS Object Model)

Important note:
- JavaScript can block HTML parsing if it is not async or defer

Example:
<script src="app.js"></script> // blocks parsing

Reflow (Layout):
Reflow is the process of calculating the position and size of elements on the page.

Triggers for reflow:
- Changing element size
- Changing layout (e.g., display, position)
- Adding/removing elements

Reflow is expensive because it may affect many elements.

Repaint:
Repaint occurs when visual styles change without affecting layout.

Examples:
- Changing color
- Changing background

Repaint is cheaper than reflow but still impacts performance.

Critical Rendering Path:
The critical rendering path includes:
1. HTML parsing → DOM creation
2. CSS parsing → CSSOM creation
3. Combine DOM + CSSOM → Render Tree
4. Layout (reflow)
5. Paint (repaint)

Goal:
Minimize the time to first render (fast page load).

Performance Tips:
- Minimize render-blocking resources
- Use async/defer for scripts
- Reduce CSS complexity
- Avoid unnecessary reflows
- Batch DOM updates

Example:
<script src="app.js" defer></script>

Page Lifecycle Events:
- DOMContentLoaded → DOM is ready
- load → all resources (images, CSS) are loaded

Example:
document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => {
  console.log('DOM ready');
});

Summary (Interview Answer):
The page lifecycle describes how a browser loads and renders a page. HTML and CSS are parsed into DOM and CSSOM, combined into a render tree, then laid out (reflow) and painted (repaint). The critical rendering path defines these steps. Reflow recalculates layout and is expensive, while repaint updates visual styles. Optimizing these processes improves performance.

Design Patterns and SOLID Principles
content:
Definition:
Design patterns are reusable solutions to common software design problems. They help write more maintainable, scalable, and readable code. SOLID principles are guidelines that improve object-oriented design and help create robust systems.

Categories of Design Patterns:

1. Creational Patterns:
These deal with object creation mechanisms.

Examples:
- Singleton: ensures only one instance exists
- Factory: creates objects without exposing creation logic
- Builder: constructs complex objects step by step

Example (Factory):
function createUser(type) {
  if (type === 'admin') return { role: 'admin' };
  return { role: 'user' };
}

2. Structural Patterns:
These focus on how objects are composed and structured.

Examples:
- Adapter: allows incompatible interfaces to work together
- Decorator: adds functionality to an object dynamically
- Facade: provides a simplified interface to a complex system

Example (Decorator):
function withLogging(fn) {
  return function(...args) {
    console.log('Calling function');
    return fn(...args);
  };
}

3. Behavioral Patterns:
These define how objects interact and communicate.

Examples:
- Observer: allows objects to subscribe to events
- Strategy: defines interchangeable algorithms
- Command: encapsulates a request as an object

Example (Observer):
const observers = [];
function subscribe(fn) {
  observers.push(fn);
}

function notify(data) {
  observers.forEach(fn => fn(data));
}

SOLID Principles:

1. Single Responsibility Principle (SRP):
A class or function should have only one responsibility.

2. Open/Closed Principle (OCP):
Code should be open for extension but closed for modification.

3. Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP):
Subtypes should be replaceable with their base types.

4. Interface Segregation Principle (ISP):
Avoid forcing objects to depend on unused methods.

5. Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP):
Depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations.

Design Patterns in JavaScript Projects:
- Modules (ES6 modules) → modular structure
- Hooks and higher-order functions → functional patterns
- Observer pattern → event systems (e.g., Redux, event listeners)
- Factory pattern → object creation logic

Important Notes:
- Patterns are guidelines, not strict rules
- Overusing patterns can lead to unnecessary complexity
- Choose patterns based on problem context

Summary (Interview Answer):
Design patterns are reusable solutions to common problems and are grouped into creational, structural, and behavioral categories. SOLID principles provide guidelines for writing maintainable and scalable code. In JavaScript, patterns like modules, observer, and factory are commonly used. The goal is to improve code structure without overcomplicating the solution.

title: Software Development Methodologies and Estimation
content:
Definition:
Software development methodologies are structured approaches used to plan, manage, and execute software projects. They define how teams collaborate, deliver features, and adapt to changes.

Waterfall:
Waterfall is a linear and sequential approach where each phase must be completed before moving to the next.

Phases:
- Requirements
- Design
- Implementation
- Testing
- Deployment

Characteristics:
- Strict structure
- Changes are difficult to introduce later
- Suitable for well-defined, stable projects

Agile:
Agile is an iterative and flexible approach focused on delivering small increments of working software.

Principles:
- Collaboration with stakeholders
- Responding to change
- Frequent delivery of working software

Scrum:
Scrum is a popular Agile framework.

Key roles:
- Product Owner → defines requirements
- Scrum Master → facilitates process
- Development Team → builds the product

Key concepts:
- Sprint (usually 1–2 weeks)
- Sprint Planning
- Daily Stand-up
- Sprint Review
- Sprint Retrospective

Kanban:
Kanban is a visual workflow management method.

Key ideas:
- Use a board with columns (To Do, In Progress, Done)
- Limit work in progress (WIP)
- Focus on continuous delivery

Example:
Tasks move across columns as work progresses.

Comparison:
- Waterfall → rigid, sequential
- Agile → flexible, iterative
- Scrum → structured Agile framework
- Kanban → continuous flow, visual tracking

Project Estimation Techniques:
Estimation helps predict how long tasks will take.

Common techniques:

1. Story Points:
Estimate complexity rather than time.

Example:
- 1 → very simple
- 5 → medium
- 13 → complex

2. Planning Poker:
Team members estimate tasks collaboratively using cards.

3. T-shirt Sizes:
Estimate tasks as XS, S, M, L, XL.

4. Time-based estimation:
Estimate in hours or days.

Important Notes:
- Estimation is approximate, not exact
- Experience improves accuracy
- Break large tasks into smaller ones for better estimates

Summary (Interview Answer):
Software development methodologies define how projects are managed and delivered. Waterfall is a linear approach, while Agile focuses on iterative development. Scrum is an Agile framework with structured roles and sprints, and Kanban uses a visual workflow with continuous delivery. Estimation techniques like story points and planning poker help teams plan work effectively.

title: Testing in Software Development (Types, Test Pyramid, TDD, BDD)
content:
Definition:
Testing in software development ensures that code works as expected, prevents bugs, and improves reliability. Different types of testing are used at different levels of the application.

Types of Testing:

1. Unit Testing:
Tests individual functions or components in isolation.

Example:
function add(a, b) { return a + b; }
// test: add(2, 3) === 5

2. Integration Testing:
Tests how different parts of the system work together.

Example:
Testing interaction between API and database.

3. End-to-End (E2E) Testing:
Tests the entire application from the user's perspective.

Example:
Simulating a user logging in and completing a flow.

4. Performance Testing:
Measures how the application performs under load.

Example:
Checking response time and scalability.

Test Pyramid:
The test pyramid is a model that suggests having more low-level tests and fewer high-level tests.

Structure:
- Bottom: Unit tests (fast, many)
- Middle: Integration tests
- Top: E2E tests (slow, few)

Reason:
- Unit tests are fast and easy to maintain
- E2E tests are slower and more complex

Test-Driven Development (TDD):
TDD is a development approach where tests are written before the actual code.

Cycle:
1. Write a failing test
2. Write minimal code to pass the test
3. Refactor the code

Benefits:
- Better design
- Fewer bugs
- High test coverage

Behavior-Driven Development (BDD):
BDD focuses on describing behavior in a human-readable way.

Example (Gherkin syntax):
Given the user is logged in
When the user clicks the button
Then the result should be displayed

Tools:
- Cucumber
- Jest (with descriptive tests)

Important Notes:
- Do not rely only on E2E tests
- Keep tests simple and focused
- Good tests improve confidence in code changes

Summary (Interview Answer):
Testing ensures software reliability and includes unit, integration, E2E, and performance testing. The test pyramid recommends many unit tests and fewer E2E tests. TDD is a process of writing tests before code, while BDD focuses on describing behavior in a readable format. Together, these practices help create stable and maintainable applications.

title: Web Communication Protocols (HTTP, HTTPS, REST)
content:
Definition:
Web communication protocols define how data is transferred between clients (browsers) and servers. The most important protocol for web development is HTTP and its secure version HTTPS.

HTTP vs HTTPS:
HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol):
- Transfers data in plain text
- Not secure
- Vulnerable to interception (man-in-the-middle attacks)

HTTPS (HTTP Secure):
- Uses SSL/TLS encryption
- Data is encrypted during transmission
- Ensures security, integrity, and authentication

Example:
http://example.com → not secure
https://example.com → secure

HTTP Methods:
HTTP methods define the type of action to perform.

Common methods:
- GET → retrieve data
- POST → send new data
- PUT → update existing data
- PATCH → partially update data
- DELETE → remove data

Example:
GET /users
POST /users

HTTP Headers:
Headers provide additional information about the request or response.

Examples:
- Content-Type: application/json
- Authorization: Bearer token
- Accept: application/json

HTTP Responses:
A response from the server contains:
- Status code
- Headers
- Body (data)

Status Codes:
Status codes indicate the result of the request.

Categories:
- 1xx → informational
- 2xx → success (200 OK, 201 Created)
- 3xx → redirection
- 4xx → client errors (400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found)
- 5xx → server errors (500 Internal Server Error)

RESTful APIs:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing APIs.

Principles:
- Stateless → each request contains all necessary information
- Resource-based → everything is treated as a resource (e.g., /users)
- Uses standard HTTP methods
- Consistent and predictable URLs

Example:
GET /users → get all users
GET /users/1 → get one user
POST /users → create user
PUT /users/1 → update user
DELETE /users/1 → delete user

Important Notes:
- HTTPS is required for modern web applications
- Proper use of HTTP methods improves API design
- REST APIs are widely used for client-server communication

Summary (Interview Answer):
HTTP is a protocol for data transfer, while HTTPS adds encryption for security. HTTP methods like GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE define actions on resources. Headers provide metadata, and status codes indicate request results. RESTful APIs use HTTP standards to create scalable and stateless communication between client and server.

title: Web Security Basics (CORS, XSS, OWASP, Authentication)
content:
Definition:
Web security focuses on protecting web applications from attacks and ensuring safe communication between client and server. Understanding common vulnerabilities and authentication methods is essential for modern development.

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing):
CORS is a browser security mechanism that restricts requests from one origin to another.

Example:
Frontend: http://localhost:3000
Backend: http://api.example.com

The browser blocks requests unless the server allows them.

Server response example:
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:3000

Important notes:
- CORS is enforced by the browser, not the server
- It protects users from malicious cross-site requests

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting):
XSS is an attack where malicious scripts are injected into a web page.

Example:
<input value="<script>alert('hack')</script>" />

Types:
- Stored XSS → saved in database
- Reflected XSS → comes from request
- DOM-based XSS → executed in browser

Prevention:
- Escape user input
- Use safe APIs (textContent instead of innerHTML)
- Validate and sanitize input

OWASP Top 10:
OWASP Top 10 is a list of the most critical web security risks.

Examples:
- Broken Authentication
- Sensitive Data Exposure
- Injection (SQL, XSS)
- Security Misconfiguration

Authentication Mechanisms:
Authentication verifies user identity.

JWT (JSON Web Token):
JWT is a token-based authentication method.

Flow:
1. User logs in
2. Server returns a token
3. Client stores token (usually in memory or storage)
4. Client sends token in headers (Authorization: Bearer token)

Advantages:
- Stateless
- Scalable

Risks:
- Token theft if not stored securely

OAuth:
OAuth is an authorization protocol that allows users to log in using third-party services.

Example:
- "Login with Google"

Flow:
1. User is redirected to provider
2. User grants permission
3. App receives access token

Difference between JWT and OAuth:
- JWT → authentication (who you are)
- OAuth → authorization (what you can access)

Important Notes:
- Always use HTTPS to protect data
- Never trust client input
- Follow OWASP guidelines for security best practices

Summary (Interview Answer):
Web security involves protecting applications from attacks like XSS and enforcing policies like CORS. OWASP Top 10 highlights common vulnerabilities developers should avoid. Authentication methods like JWT provide stateless user verification, while OAuth allows secure third-party authorization. Proper validation, sanitization, and secure communication are essential for building safe applications.
